title: Digital Quantum Simulation of the quantum $β$-FPUT Lattice: Formulation and Resource Estimation
authors: Kiratholly Nandakumar Madhav Sharma, Juan Manuel Aguiar Hualde, Julian van Velzen, Phalgun Lolur
abstract: Heat conduction in low-dimensional systems exhibits strong deviations from Fourier behavior due to anharmonicity and long-lived vibrational correlations, challenging conventional computational approaches. The $β$-Fermi--Pasta--Ulam--Tsingou ($β$-FPUT) chain provides a minimal nonlinear lattice model for studying anomalous transport, yet its quantum real-time dynamics remain difficult to access with classical methods. We develop a first-quantized digital quantum-simulation framework for the quantum $β$-FPUT lattice, targeting fault-tolerant quantum computers. By working directly with discretized lattice displacements rather than truncated phonon occupation spaces, the approach captures anharmonic interactions while avoiding bosonic encoding overheads. We construct Trotterized circuit blocks for real-time evolution and introduce a Hermitian quadrature decomposition of Fourier-mode displacement operators that enables shallow quantum circuits for mode-resolved displacement correlators. We analyze the quantum resources required for the full simulation and measurement workflow, providing qubit counts, gate complexity, circuit-depth and resource estimates as functions of system size and resolution within a fault-tolerant workflow. These results establish a concrete algorithmic blueprint for simulating quantum transport dynamics in nonlinear low-dimensional lattice models on fault-tolerant quantum hardware.
summary: This paper presents a new method for simulating the quantum beta-FPUT lattice on future fault-tolerant quantum computers. By using a first-quantized approach and a clever decomposition of displacement operators, the authors reduce the circuit complexity and provide a detailed estimate of the necessary qubits and gates. This work establishes a blueprint for studying complex heat transport and phonon dynamics in nonlinear low-dimensional systems.
main_problem: Developing an efficient digital quantum simulation framework for the quantum beta-FPUT lattice and estimating the resources required for fault-tolerant implementation.
main_result: The authors provide a concrete algorithmic blueprint and resource roadmap, demonstrating that a first-quantized approach using quadrature decomposition enables shallow circuits for simulating nonlinear transport dynamics.
method: A first-quantized digital quantum simulation using Trotterized circuit blocks, a Hermitian quadrature decomposition of Fourier-mode displacement operators, and a Hadamard-test-based measurement workflow.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional quantum beta-Fermi-Pasta-Ulam-Tsingou (beta-FPUT) lattice, characterized by a Hamiltonian with kinetic energy and a potential containing both quadratic and quartic anharmonic terms.
key_observables: Mode-resolved displacement correlators and quantum dynamical observables relevant to phonon lifetimes.
important_parameters: Lattice size (N), grid resolution (bits per site), mass (m), harmonic coupling (kappa), anharmonicity (beta), and Trotter step size (delta t).
main_assumptions: The framework assumes fault-tolerant quantum hardware rather than NISQ-era devices and assumes periodic boundary conditions for the lattice.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows motivation regarding heat dissipation in microelectronics and bio-devices; Figure 2 depicts the 1D FPUT chain; Figure 4 illustrates a two-site potential-layer circuit; Figure 5 shows circuits for implementing cosine and sine quadrature unitaries.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from physical motivation and the mathematical formulation of the beta-FPUT model to the construction of the quantum simulation framework, followed by error analysis, resource estimation, and a comparison with hardware roadmaps.
why_interesting: It provides a scalable algorithmic approach to simulating nonlinear many-body dynamics and anomalous transport, specifically addressing how to bypass the high overheads of bosonic encoding in quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Conceptual motivation for studying thermal transport in low- dimensional systems.
Fig. 2. Schematic diagram of the one-dimensional FPUT chain with sites labeled {0, 1, 2, . . . , N −1}.

Fig. 4. Two-site potential-layer skeleton implementing subtraction, controlled phase kickback, and inverse subtraction. Here, SUB computes the relative displacement ∆0 = s1 −s0 into the ancilla register without disturbing s0 and s1, while UNSUB uncomputes it. The controlled phase gates are Φ2 = exp[−i(κ/2)∆2 0∆t/ℏ] and Φ4 = exp[−i(β/4)∆4 0∆t/ℏ], acting on the reference qubit r0 and controlled by the ancilla.

Fig. 3. Kinetic half-step circuit. Each n-qubit QFTn is decomposed into Hadamard and controlled-phase gates cRℓ. The exact QFT requires O(n2) two-qubit gates, while approximate QFT reduces the count by truncating small-angle rotations. The diagonal operator Dp(∆t/2) applies the phase e−ip2 j ∆t/(4mℏ) in the momentum basis via phase kickback using reversible arithmetic followed by uncomputation.

Fig. 5. Quantum circuits implementing eiθ ˆ Q(c) k (left) and eiθ ˆ Q(s) k (right). Each qubit qj,r is rotated by an angle ϕ(c) j,r or ϕ(s) j,r from Eq. (35). Since all rotations commute, the operations can be parallelized, producing depth-two circuits.
